lesson: Caching zobristKey results in a Map eliminates redundant fnv1a64 string hashing. High cache hit rate because most game state is unchanged between moves.

Trace/debug infrastructure (effectPath strings, withTracePath context spreads, resolveTraceProvenance objects, emitTrace argument construction) is a significant hidden cost when called 100K+ times. Skip ALL trace-related work when tracing is disabled — check the trace-enabled flag BEFORE doing any string construction, object creation, or context spreading.

V8 JIT optimizes object spreads, regex, and BigInt XOR efficiently. Micro-optimizations that try to avoid these patterns (Object.create(null), manual string parsing, split bigint pairs) are consistently SLOWER due to overhead exceeding savings or breaking V8 hidden classes.

Deep profiling before guessing prevents wasted experiments. The assumed bottleneck (setVar state spreads at 127ms) was dwarfed by the actual bottleneck (phase lifecycle effects at 17000ms, agent double-validation at 10000ms, listPlayers array allocation at 372K calls). ALWAYS profile first — add opt-in instrumentation to any depth.

Agent preparePlayableMoves was calling legalChoicesEvaluate + evaluatePlayableMoveCandidate (double-validation) for every legal move. Replacing with single-pass probeMoveViability eliminated 53% of agent time. Redundant validation across call boundaries is the #1 optimization target.

resolvePlayerSel called listPlayers (Array.from allocation) on EVERY call including the fast-path 'chosen' selector. With 372K pvar lookups, this created 372K unnecessary arrays. Fast-pathing the chosen selector with a bounds check instead saved 3.6%. Look for hidden allocations inside high-frequency selector/resolver functions.

V8 hidden class monomorphism is critical. Fast-paths that create objects with different property shapes at the same call site cause catastrophic deoptimization (17% regression in exp-026). Even different return points in the SAME function with the SAME property keys create polymorphism. Only optimize within the existing object creation pattern.

Functions called on every move (like dispatchTriggers) that are no-ops for certain game configurations (0 triggers) should fast-path before doing any work. The array copy [...triggerLog], resource creation, and graph resolution in dispatchTriggers were ~630ms of wasted work across 50 games.

advanceToDecisionPoint called legalMoves() just to check .length > 0, enumerating ALL legal moves across all actions. Adding earlyExitAfterFirst option stops after finding the first legal move, saving 3.39%. Pattern: any existence check that calls an enumerate-all function should use an early-exit variant.

computeAlwaysCompleteActionIds excluded parameterized actions, forcing probeMoveViability probes for every raise legal move (~10K probes/game). Actions with params ARE always-complete if their effect tree has no decision effects (chooseOne/chooseN/rollRandom). Removing the params.length > 0 exclusion saved 10.61%. LESSON: overly conservative exclusion logic can create massive hidden costs in hot paths.

Passing a profiler object through the kernel hot path caused 34% overhead — NOT from the profiling calls themselves but from V8 JIT deoptimization. The profiler field on EffectContext created polymorphic object shapes that prevented V8 from inlining and optimizing hot path functions. Stripping the profiler from kernel options in the simulator (keeping only sim-level timing) saved 23.94%. CRITICAL LESSON: never thread optional diagnostic objects through hot-path function signatures. Keep instrumentation at the boundary, not throughout the call graph.

buildIntRangeCandidates built full 960-element arrays for raise amount ranges that were immediately downsampled to 10. Direct mathematical computation of the downsampled subset (O(maxResults * required) vs O(range)) eliminated 41.8% of legalMoves time. Pattern: when selecting a small subset from a large range, compute the subset directly instead of materializing the full range.

evalValue concat handler used .map() + .filter() + .map().join() = 3 intermediate arrays per call. With 100K+ concat evals, this was 300K+ unnecessary array allocations (41% of CPU). Replacing with single-pass for loop + string concatenation saved 27.95%. ALSO: aggregate handler used .map() with per-item { ...ctx, bindings: { ...ctx.bindings, [bind]: item } } = 2 spreads per item. Replacing with mutable bindings object (safe for synchronous evalValue) eliminated all per-item spreads. CRITICAL LESSON: .map().filter().reduce() chains in hot paths are allocation bombs. Always use for loops in performance-critical code.

resolveBindingTemplate ran a regex /\{([^{}]+)\}/g on EVERY call. In Texas Hold'em, 100% of templates are plain strings with no {binding} placeholders. A single indexOf('{') === -1 check eliminated 20.54% of total runtime. CRITICAL LESSON: regex-based string processing in hot paths MUST have a fast-path for the common case (no matches). This will also benefit FITL if many of its template strings are plain.

Inlining logic into evalValue (exp-014: switch dispatch + inline validation) caused V8 JIT deoptimization — the function became too large for inlining. Lazy closures (exp-015) were MORE expensive than objects. V8 is extremely sensitive to function size in the 100K+ call-per-game range. LESSON: for ultra-hot functions like evalValue (~2M calls/game), do NOT make the function body larger. Keep it small and delegate to helper functions that V8 can inline individually.

After interpreter optimizations (mutable context, concat/aggregate loops, binding template short-circuit, profiler removal), the compiled lifecycle effect path became 12-16% SLOWER than the interpreter. The compiled path's per-fragment overhead (normalizeFragmentResult, context spreading) exceeds the interpreter's optimized per-effect overhead (mutable workCtx, direct dispatch). LESSON: compiled effect paths need to be re-benchmarked after interpreter optimizations — they may no longer provide a net benefit.

In resolveRef, 'binding' refs are the most common type in effect chains but were handled last in a 14-way if-else chain. Moving the binding check to the first position eliminated ~700K unnecessary string comparisons per game, saving 3.51%. Pattern: profile which discriminant is most frequent in hot-path dispatch chains and put it first.

Nested effect scopes (if/let/forEach calling applyEffectsWithBudgetState) re-created mutable state clones + DraftTracker Sets even though the parent scope already had a mutable state. Checking cursor.tracker to skip redundant createMutableState/createDraftTracker saved 8%. Pattern: any scope-boundary setup that is idempotent should check if the parent already did it.

createSeatResolutionContext rebuilds 5 Maps + arrays from scratch on every call. For FITL with 30K+ probeMoveViability calls per benchmark, this was the #1 redundant computation. A WeakMap cache keyed on def.seats (stable reference) with playerCount validation eliminated 6.31% of runtime. Pattern: any function that builds Maps/Sets from GameDef fields and is called per-move or per-probe should use WeakMap caching.

Adding ANY new field to EffectCursor or GameDefRuntime interfaces causes V8 hidden class deoptimization (4-7% regression). Even optional fields that are always present within a scope break monomorphism. This was proven across 5 experiments (exp-003: pipelineActionIds on GameDefRuntime, exp-006: triggerIndex on GameDefRuntime, exp-013: arena on EffectCursor). CRITICAL: never add properties to hot-path object interfaces for optimization purposes.

Import aliasing (import { X as Y }) in hot-path modules causes V8 JIT deoptimization (6.82% regression). V8 inlines based on exact function identity; aliased imports change the identity at the call site, breaking monomorphic dispatch. Never rename exports via aliased imports in performance-critical modules.

Branch reordering for early-exit optimization (moving phase check before contract evaluation) caused 4% regression in FITL. V8 JIT optimizes branch prediction based on the ORIGINAL ordering profile. Also, for...in iteration is consistently slower than Set-based iteration in V8 (4.2% regression). V8 prefers typed collection iteration over property enumeration.

advanceToDecisionPoint calls legalMoves(earlyExitAfterFirst) in a loop. Checking trivial actions (no params, no pre, always-complete) FIRST when earlyExitAfterFirst eliminates O(actions) per iteration. For FITL: 41% improvement.

V8 hidden class deoptimization extends to ALL frequently-created objects in the kernel hot path (MoveEnumerationState, EffectCursor, GameDefRuntime, ReadContext). Adding ANY field causes 2-7% regression. Also: WeakMap/Map caching, frozen array iteration, and closure variable capture all cause V8 deopt in this codebase.

96% of FITL legalMoves cost is ENUMERATION (action preflights + pipeline predicate evaluation + decision sequence admission), only 4% is classification probes. Sim-level profiling attributed this to legalMoves total, hiding that the bottleneck is inside the enumeration effect execution calls, not the classification step.

Probes (probeMoveViability) are CHEAPER than agent completions (evaluatePlayableMoveCandidate). Probe stops at first decision (~1ms), completion resolves ALL decisions (~5-10ms). Never bypass probes to shift work to agents — 5-10x cost increase per move.

resolvePlayerSel called listPlayers + sortAndDedupePlayers for 'actor' and 'active' selectors that always resolve to a single known player ID. Fast-pathing to return [playerId] directly avoids listPlayers array allocation, Set creation, array spread, and sort — 4 allocations per call. With 15K+ calls per benchmark, saved 1.92%. Pattern: any selector resolving to a single known value should skip collection infrastructure.

Early exits in CALLEE functions work for hot-path optimization, even when modifying the CALLER's control flow fails. evaluateActionSelectorContracts had an early-exit check before Set creation that saved 1.11%. The key insight: V8 JIT optimizes the caller based on its branch structure. Changes inside callees don't disrupt the caller's JIT optimization. Modifying hot-path caller control flow causes V8 deopt, but modifying callee return paths does not.

WeakSet.has() as an 'already validated' cache is profitable when the validation cost exceeds the hash lookup cost AND the validation callee is in the PREFLIGHT path (not the kernel execution path). assertEvalRuntimeResourcesContract validated the same object 25 times per legalMoves call. A single WeakSet.has() check saved 1.48%. Contrasts with exp-012 where WeakSet on GameState inside legalChoicesDiscover caused +12.9% regression. Pattern: use WeakSet for idempotent validation caching in PREFLIGHT callees only.

Agent behavior changes (completionsPerTemplate, decision parameters) are NOT safe performance optimizations. Different move selections produce different game state trajectories with different legalMoves complexity. In FITL, reducing completions from 3 to 2 made the agent 27% faster but caused 16% slower legalMoves due to harder game states. Also: adding cross-module imports between agents/ and kernel/ hot-path modules causes catastrophic V8 deoptimization (13% regression from a single import).

Kernel computation function modifications are UNIFORMLY unsafe: direct construction replacing spread (exp-010: +8%), spread removal (exp-011: +11%), early exits in computation callees (exp-002: +2%, exp-007: +6%). V8 JIT has deeply profiled the kernel's object construction and iteration patterns. The ONLY safe modifications are to VALIDATION callees that perform no actual computation. ANY change to computation callees or construction patterns triggers hidden class polymorphism and JIT deopt.

resolveActionActorCore was creating eval context + calling resolvePlayerSel for every action preflight, but when action.actor === active (100% of FITL actions), the result is always applicable since decisionPlayer === activePlayer. A simple string equality check fast-path eliminated ~9000 unnecessary object allocations per game. Pattern: validation callees that check trivially-true properties of common selector values should short-circuit before any object creation.

Adding early returns to SMALL utility functions (like isWithinActionLimits, ~10 lines) that are called from hot-path callees causes catastrophic V8 deoptimization (+19% regression). V8 inlines small functions into their callers. Changing the inlined body changes the caller optimization profile. ONLY modify callees that are too large for V8 inlining (~60+ lines, like resolveActionActorCore at 30+ lines with try/catch). Small utility functions are effectively part of the caller at JIT time.

findAction in legal-choices.ts did def.actions.find() — O(n) linear scan through all actions — called 25+ times per legalMoves invocation via decision sequence discovery. A WeakMap-cached Map<ActionId, ActionDef> keyed on def.actions array (stable reference) saved 1.98%. Pattern: look for Array.find() and Array.some() calls on GameDef arrays (def.actions, def.zones, etc.) that execute inside the enumeration loop. These are hidden O(n) costs that multiply with call frequency.

Boosting VC's economic engine actions (Rally=3, Tax=2) over combat actions (weight 1) improved compositeScore by 16.2%. The VC victory formula (Opposition + Bases > 35) rewards building infrastructure over fighting. Simple action weight differentiation is high-leverage.

Weight-only changes (Tier 1) hit a ceiling when action-type ranking is already established. Within the same action type, candidates may score identically if no candidate-level features differentiate them. Candidate-level differentiation comes from working preview (projectedSelfMargin), completionScoreTerms, and candidateFeatures. Preview was fixed via specs 96-97 (decision-point state snapshots) and now provides real candidate-level differentiation — projectedMarginWeight=5 is the strongest scoring signal.

dropPassWhenOtherMovesExist pruning rule is ESSENTIAL for all profiles. Without it, pass scores 0 and ties with unscored actions; stableMoveKey picks pass alphabetically. When all players pass, the game never terminates (infinite loop). NEVER remove this rule.

Action weight values are a threshold effect: as long as the RANKING is Rally > Tax > Event > unscored, the exact numeric values don't matter. Rally=2 and Rally=5 produce identical outcomes. This is because most decision points have few candidates and the action-type ranking determines the choice, not the magnitude.

Preview system was fixed via specs 96-97 (decision-point state snapshots, global state aggregation). Previous attempts to bypass preview at the template level failed because moves lacked inner decisions. The architectural fix (state snapshots at decision points) resolved the root cause. Preview now evaluates candidates successfully (evaluatedCandidateCount > 0, unknownRefs: []) and projectedSelfMargin provides real candidate-level differentiation across action types.

candidate.paramCount (number of resolved decisions in a move) is NOT a good proxy for move quality. More params means more target zones for Rally, but spreading guerrillas across many zones is often worse than concentrating in strategic locations. Tiebreaker changes cascade through entire game trajectories across all seeds.

VC macro-strategy Rally>Tax>Event is near-optimal and MUST NOT be inverted. Tax-first causes catastrophic failure (games hitting maxTurns, margin -12). The VC economic engine is Rally-driven: guerrillas -> bases -> opposition. Tax is fuel for Rally, not a standalone strategy. Further improvement requires micro-level candidate differentiation, not macro-level ranking changes.

candidateParam for pipeline choice bindings ($noBaseChoice, $withBaseChoice, $targetSpaces) requires suffix/index matching in resolveCandidateParam. Direct lookup fails because move.params keys include full decision paths. The fix enables future experiments but candidate-level features may not TRIGGER if game conditions for certain options don't arise in the evaluation seed set.

completionScoreTerms with zoneProp enable candidate-level differentiation that breaks the action-type-weight ceiling. zoneProp returns 'unknown' type at compile time — must wrap in coalesce with a numeric fallback (e.g., coalesce: [zoneProp: {zone: {ref: option.value}, prop: population}, 0]) to satisfy the score term number type requirement. This pattern works for any zone attribute (population, econ, etc).

candidateParam ref syntax is `candidate.param.X` (not `candidateParam.X`). candidateFeatures using candidate.param.X fire for ALL move candidates that have parameter X, not just a specific action type. Must use `when: eq(feature.isActionName, true)` to restrict. Also: population-based scoring of agitate is WRONG — high-pop zones cost more to agitate in FITL, making them suboptimal targets.

Pipeline decision-sequence satisfiability probes (isMoveDecisionSequenceAdmittedForLegalMove) are redundant for template moves when the compiled first-decision domain check already validates the first decision. The probe executes the full pipeline effect tree (applyChooseN, evalQuery, filterTokensByExpr) just to verify satisfiability — work the agent handles gracefully at completion time via templateCompletionUnsatisfiable. Removing the probe saved 4.7% of total runtime. Pattern: defer validation to the consumer when the consumer has robust error handling.

FITL kernel computation functions (resolveRef, evalCondition, foldTokenFilterExpr, matchesScalarMembership) are at a V8 JIT optimization ceiling. ANY modification — WeakMap caching (+7.7%), fast-path branching (+3.8%), short-circuit evaluation (test failures) — causes hidden class deoptimization. The ONLY safe optimization pattern is removing WORK at the orchestration level (skipping calls entirely), not modifying how calls behave.

After exhausting local optimizations, the remaining FITL performance bottleneck is architectural: (1) object spread immutability costs 15% CPU — addressable via Foundation 11's scoped-draft-state carve-out, (2) interpretive AST evaluation costs 19% — addressable via AOT expression compilation, (3) string-based ID comparisons cost 9% — addressable via integer-interned domain IDs. These require spec-level architectural changes, not campaign-level experiments.

projectedMarginWeight=5 with working preview is the strongest scoring signal for VC agent. Reducing it from 5 to 1 caused -42.6% compositeScore regression (3.1333 -> 1.8). The preview system evaluates all non-pass candidates (evaluatedCandidateCount matches legal moves) and provides different projected margins per action type (e.g., terror=-30, attack=-40, event varies -60 to -30). Do NOT reduce projectedMarginWeight — it IS the primary decision driver.

Event preview DOES differentiate shaded vs unshaded card sides after Spec 109 implementation. Card-68 shaded scored -30 (margin -6) while unshaded scored -40 to -60 (margin -8 to -12). Preview correctly evaluates event quality. The prior belief that events were blind was based on pre-Spec-109 traces.

With working preview (projectedMarginWeight=5), all action-type weight considerations (preferEvent, preferTaxWeighted, preserveResources) are dead weight — preview correctly ranks actions by projected margin. Only preferRallyWeighted is essential (captures multi-turn infrastructure value preview can't see). The minimal VC profile is: preferProjectedSelfMargin + preferRallyWeighted + penalizeAttack + preferPopulousTargets + dropPassWhenOtherMovesExist + tiebreakers.

Tiny targeted PENALTIES (-0.1) work where bonuses fail. penalizeAttack(-0.1) flipped 2 margin-0 seeds to wins by redirecting tied attack→terror decisions. Key: penalty only affects ties (negligible vs 5-point preview weight), and attack-terror ties exist on losing seeds but not winning seeds (asymmetric effect). Action-type BONUSES (even 0.5) cascade catastrophically via butterfly effect on winning seeds.

Card annotations (activeCard.annotation.<side>.<metric>) are compiled for all 130 FITL event cards but annotation-based bonuses (w=2-3) cannot overcome the 20-point preview gap for capability/operation-granting events. At margin -8: terror projects -4 (score -20), capability event projects -8 (score -40). Bonus of +2-3 barely dents this. Multi-step preview (Spec 111) is needed to correctly value events that grant free operations.

DSL type nuance: activeCard.hasTag.* compiles to number (0/1), not boolean. activeCard.annotation.* compiles to number. globalMarker.* compiles to 'id' (string). Use type: number for tag/annotation features, type: id for marker features, gt: [ref, 0] for when clauses, coalesce with 0 (not false). boolToNumber requires boolean input — don't wrap tag refs in it.

Signal normalization is ESSENTIAL for multi-signal agents. Raw preview margin (range ±50) dominates any additive bonus (range 0-5). Use candidateAggregates with min/max to compute the margin range, then div in the consideration's value expression to normalize to [0,1]. With normalized margin at weight 5 (range 0-5), secondary signals (capabilities w=3, rally w=3) compete fairly. Normalization enabled +2 wins in FITL campaign (compositeScore 2.4667→3.6). IMPORTANT: normalize in considerations, not candidateFeatures (candidateFeatures cannot reference aggregates).

Duplicate candidates with identical stableMoveKey exist in FITL candidate lists (31 candidates, 15 unique). Duplicates arise from template completion exploring multiple paths to the same resolved move. No dedup step exists in preparePlayableMoves. Duplicates get preview: undefined (preview cache serves first occurrence only), distort normalized scoring aggregates, and waste computation. Spec 114 filed.

Surface ref type bug: compile-agents.ts:1813 hardcoded ALL surface refs as type 'number', but globalMarker.* returns string (marker state) and activeCard.id returns string (card identity). Fixed to return 'id' for globalMarker and activeCardIdentity families, 'unknown' for activeCardMetadata. Without this fix, eq(globalMarker.X, 'shaded') fails type checking.

Rally dominance is the winning VC strategy. Any non-Rally action preference (Terror, Events, Tax) causes regression across seeds. The Rally-first strategy builds guerrilla/base infrastructure that enables effective Coup agitation — the primary margin-gaining mechanism.

Normalized margin scoring outperforms raw margin at scale. Raw margin worked better at 3 seeds but caused regression at 10+ seeds because Rally's bonus (weight 5) couldn't compete with raw margin differences (range -40 to +10). Normalized margin compresses the range to [0,1], letting secondary signals (Rally, capability) compete fairly.

Features that test as dead weight at low seed counts may become essential at higher seed counts. valueCapabilityGain and preferPopulousTargets were removed as simplifications at tier 3 (3 seeds) but re-adding them at tier 15 each flipped an additional seed from loss/draw to win.

Opponent margin awareness via preview.victory.currentMargin.<seatName> is supported but cannot prevent structurally unwinnable seeds. US jumps from margin -13 to +1 entirely through Coup pacification (multi-step post-decision process). No pre-Coup VC action can prevent this swing. Defensive play slightly degrades winning seed margins without flipping any losses.

Enriched tournament traces (all-seat margins, opponent moves, per-seat action summaries) are essential for data-driven agent evolution. Without them, the campaign spent ~20 experiments guessing at opponent behavior. With them, structural unwinnability was proven in 1 experiment.

completionPolicy: required on free operation grants can deadlock the game when no legal completion exists (template completion fails). FITL Card 75 (Sihanoukville shaded, march from rally zones) triggers this on seed 1009. Needs skipIfNoLegalCompletion policy. Filed as FREOPSKIP-001.

Double function calls in ternary conditions (e.g., f().length > 0 ? f() : default) and double-call patterns (f() === undefined ? {} : { key: f() }) are low-hanging fruit in orchestration loops. Caching in a local variable is zero-risk and can yield 6%+ when the functions have non-trivial cost. Also, replacing .some() predicates with Set.has() for membership checks on stable collections eliminates O(n) per call.

Returning a singleton vs freshly-constructed object from the same function causes V8 JIT deoptimization (+6.5%). V8 inline caches at call sites assume consistent object identity patterns — polymorphic returns (sometimes pre-existing reference, sometimes new) poison the cache even when shapes match. Creating new Map() inside a function that V8 has profiled also causes deopt (+3.6%). The ONLY safe allocation reduction in this codebase is eliminating calls entirely (exp-001/002 pattern), not changing how existing calls return values.
